did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a convolutional neural network (CNN) to classify dermoscopic images into 32 categories of melanoma. The methodology involved training a 3-level CNN using the Adam optimizer on a dataset of 2013 melanoma images, achieving a validation accuracy of 88-97%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a 3-level convolutional neural network (CNN) to classify dermoscopic images into 32 categories. It details specific methodology (Adam optimizer, 70 epochs, 45 batches), uses a specific dataset (2013 images from Kawahara et al.), and reports new results including validation accuracy (88-97%) and confusion matrix metrics. It is published in the proceedings of the 16th International Workshop on Semantic and Social Media Adaptation & Personalization (SMAP).

title: Applied Deep learning for categorizing dermoscopic images
journal: 16th International Workshop on Semantic and Social Media Adaptation & Personalization (SMAP)
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning approach using a 3-level convolutional neural network (CNN) to classify dermoscopic images into one of 32 melanoma categories. The model was trained and validated on a dataset of 2013 melanoma images sourced from Kawahara et al. The CNN architecture consisted of 2D convolutional layers with ReLU activation, max-pooling, and dropout layers, optimized using the Adam optimizer. Training was conducted over 70 epochs with 45 batches per epoch, resulting in a validation accuracy ranging from 88% to 97%. The results demonstrate that the proposed CNN model is reliable for melanoma diagnosis and offers competitive performance compared to existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies dermoscopic images into one of 32 categories of melanoma using a dataset of 2013 images from Kawahara et al. The paper mentions basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) only for context in the introduction, noting they are less common but less dangerous than melanoma.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a convolutional neural network to classify dermoscopic images into one out of 32 categories of melanoma. The dataset contains 2013 images from different categories of melanoma. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma subtypes) rather than continuous values. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score derived from a confusion matrix.

datasets_used.name: Kawahara et al. dataset
datasets_used.description: An existing dataset containing dermoscopic images of melanoma, sourced from Kawahara et al. [10]. The paper notes it contains images divided into 34 categories (though the study focuses on 32 categories).

use.training: Used for training the convolutional neural network (CNN) over 70 epochs with 45 batches per epoch.
use.validation: Used for validation, with reported accuracy ranging from 88% to 97%.
use.testing: A subset of 32 random melanoma images from different categories was used for evaluation/testing to generate a confusion matrix and metrics.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To extract classification metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, F1-score) from predictions on a subset of 32 random melanoma images.
Used as the optimization algorithm for model training, with accuracy used as the metric to indicate effectiveness.
Used to avoid overfitting during the training process.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score derived from a confusion matrix on a subset of 32 images, and compared descriptively with other works in Table I.

Kawahara et al. dataset (ISBI 2016/2017).notes: The model was trained on 2013 images from 34 categories (focused on 32). Training was performed over 70 epochs with 45 batches per epoch using the Adam optimizer. The validation accuracy ranged from 88% to 97%.

Evaluation Subset (32 random melanoma im.notes: Results from 10 sample executions on a subset of 32 random melanoma images. A 32x32 confusion matrix was formulated to extract these metrics. The paper also reports a comparative accuracy of 90% and sensitivity of 87% for the overall model in Table I.

Comparative Results (Table I).notes: Table I compares the proposed model's performance against other works. The proposed model achieved 90% accuracy and 87% sensitivity.

architecture_details.structure: 3-level network where each level consists of a convolutional layer, max-pooling layer, and dropout layer. The convolutional layer reduces the image size to half in each execution. A flatten layer connects the previous layers to a fully connected (dense) layer.

conclusions: The proposed 3-level convolutional neural network (CNN) is reliable for the diagnosis of melanoma, achieving a validation accuracy ranging from 88% to 97%.
The model provides competitive performance compared to existing methods, achieving 90% accuracy and 87% sensitivity in comparative analysis.
Future work will focus on implementing experiments on better hardware with more powerful GPUs to decrease processing time.
Future improvements may involve combining convolutional networks and experimenting with larger datasets to enhance performance.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, relatively small dataset (2013 images from Kawahara et al.) without external validation, limiting generalizability.
Evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score) were derived from a very small subset of only 32 random melanoma images, which is insufficient for robust statistical assessment.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported performance metrics.
The dataset contains images from 34 categories, but the model was trained to classify only 32 categories, creating a potential mismatch or exclusion bias not fully detailed.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may contribute to overfitting given the small dataset size, despite the use of dropout layers.
The model was trained on Google Colab, a cloud-based resource, which may introduce variability in hardware reproducibility compared to standardized local environments.

The comparative analysis in Table I mixes results from different datasets and years, making direct performance comparisons with prior works potentially unfair or misleading due to dataset heterogeneity.
The paper acknowledges that future work requires better hardware and larger datasets, indicating current limitations in scalability and performance ceiling.
